EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent winter storm rang in the New Year across Wyoming and brought heavy snow across much of the area, leading to numerous road closures, including Interstates 25 and 80,  and severely hampering holiday travel. The heaviest snow fell East of the Divide. In the Wind River Mountains, as much as 27 inches of new snow fell. The Lander area saw the highest snowfall totals in the populated areas, with anywhere from 15 to 22 inches around town. Over a foot of snow also fell in the lower elevations of Lincoln, Natrona, Sublette and Sweetwater Counties. The snow was also accompanied by wind gusts close to 50 mph in the favored locations, bring near zero visibility with blowing and drifting snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 14 inches of new snow fell at the Jackson Hole Ski Resort.

A deep fetch of Pacific moisture from an atmospheric river and an Arctic Front dropping south combined to bring very heavy snow to much of western and central Wyoming. The heaviest snow fell across the western mountains, especially in the favored westerly flow upslope locations. The highest snowfall amounts were in the Tetons, with the Jackson Hole Ski Resort reporting 47 inches of new snow. Portions of the Salt and Wyoming Range also picked up over 2 feet of new snow, with over a foot of new snow in the Bighorn Range and Wind River Range. In the lower elevations, over a foot of new snow fell in the Jackson Valley, Star Valley and Natrona County, with much of northern Wyoming seeing over 6 inches of new snow. The snow combined with gusty winds to produce widespread travel problems including the closure of several major roads for several days, disrupting commerce.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in the western Bighorn mountains, with a maximum amount of 21 inches.

The highest snowfall amounts were in the Tetons, with the Jackson Hole Ski Resort reporting 47 inches of new snow. Portions of the Salt and Wyoming Range also picked up over 2 feet of new snow, with over a foot of new snow in the Bighorn Range and Wind River Range. In the lower elevations, over a foot of new snow fell in the Jackson Valley, Star Valley and Natrona County, with much of northern Wyoming seeing over 6 inches of new snow. The snow combined with gusty winds to produce widespread travel problems including the closure of several major roads for several days, disrupting commerce.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell across much of Natrona County, with the highest amounts in the foothills of Casper Mountain where 17 inches of snow fell. Snowfall amounts generally decreased to the north and west of Casper. The heavy snow resulted in the closure of Interstate 25 and US 20 26 for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest winds on the back side of a cold frontal passage formed over Platte County in Wyoming with gusts 45 to 55 mph. A standing wave developed in the foothills of the North Laramie Range that resulted in wind gusts up to 63 mph along the I-25 corridor between Glendo and Wheatland for a 2-hour period early in the morning of February 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: WYDOT mesonet station Coleman (WY1) along Interstate 25 measured maximum wind gust 13 miles south of Glendo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent area of low pressure passed to the south of Wyoming and brought very heavy snow to portions of Wyoming. The heaviest snow fell in a band through Lincoln, Sweetwater, Fremont and Natrona Counties. The most notable snow was in Casper in Natrona County. Two snowfall records were broken. A storm total snowfall of 37.4 inches over 3 calendar days, April 3rd through April 5th, fell at the Casper airport. In addition, an all-time 1-calendar-day snowfall record was set on April 3rd with a 26.7 inches of snow. Both these numbers broke the old records which occurred during the December 23-24, 1982 snowstorm. Other notable snowfall amounts included 20 inches at Afton in Lincoln County, 60 inches of new snow on Casper Mountain and 19 inches in Green River in Sweetwater County. In Fremont County, 48 inches of new snow fell at Atlantic City in the Wind River Mountains. In the lower elevations, 7 to 11 inches of new snow fell around Riverton with up to 18 inches near Lander. The snow was accompanied by gusty wind in the favored locations which brought blizzard conditions at times. As a result of the heavy snow, most major roads in the central and southern portions of the state, including Interstates 25 and 80, where closed for multiple days during the storm. Many schools were closed or went to virtual learning during the height of the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy snow fell along the eastern slopes of the Wind River Range. The heaviest snow fell at Atlantic City where 4 feet of new snow fell over 48 hour period. Many SNOTEL locations reported more than 3 feet of new snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A record setting snowstorm occurred in Casper. Both daily and storm record snowfall records were broken at Casper. All areas of Casper received at least 2 feet of snow with a maximum of almost 40 inches on the south side of Casper. Amounts dropped further west and north with 14 inches near Powder River and 7 inches at Midwest. The heavy snow combined with gusty wind resulted in nearly impossible travel conditions for several days. A storm total snowfall of 37.4 inches over 3 calendar days fell at the Casper Airport, April 3rd through April 5th. In addition, an all-time 1-calendar-day snowfall record was set on April 3rd with a 26.7 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm impacted the area, bringing significant snowfall, gusty winds, and a surge of Arctic air. A series of upper-level disturbances associated with the system overrode the Arctic air at the surface and produced snowfall for an extended period. The Arctic cold front dropped temperatures to near or below zero for an extended period. Snowfall amounts were in the six to 12 inch range across a good portion of northeastern Wyoming, with amounts tailing off some across far northeastern Wyoming. Minor blowing and drifting of snow occurred with breezy conditions. Wind chill values dropped to 20 below to 35 below zero during the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm impacted the area, bringing significant snowfall, gusty winds, and a surge of Arctic air. A series of upper-level disturbances associated with the system overrode the Arctic air at the surface and produced snowfall for an extended period. The Arctic cold front dropped temperatures to near or below zero for an extended period. Snowfall amounts were in the six to 12 inch range across a good portion of northeastern Wyoming, with amounts tailing off some across far northeastern Wyoming. Minor blowing and drifting of snow occurred with breezy conditions. Wind chill values dropped to 20 below to 35 below zero during the event.

A potent area of low pressure passed to the south of Wyoming and brought very heavy snow to portions of Wyoming. The heaviest snow fell in a band through Lincoln, Sweetwater, Fremont and Natrona Counties. The most notable snow was in Casper in Natrona County. Two snowfall records were broken. A storm total snowfall of 37.4 inches over 3 calendar days, April 3rd through April 5th, fell at the Casper airport. In addition, an all-time 1-calendar-day snowfall record was set on April 3rd with a 26.7 inches of snow. Both these numbers broke the old records which occurred during the December 23-24, 1982 snowstorm. Other notable snowfall amounts included 20 inches at Afton in Lincoln County, 60 inches of new snow on Casper Mountain and 19 inches in Green River in Sweetwater County. In Fremont County, 48 inches of new snow fell at Atlantic City in the Wind River Mountains. In the lower elevations, 7 to 11 inches of new snow fell around Riverton with up to 18 inches near Lander. The snow was accompanied by gusty wind in the favored locations which brought blizzard conditions at times. As a result of the heavy snow, most major roads in the central and southern portions of the state, including Interstates 25 and 80, where closed for multiple days during the storm. Many schools were closed or went to virtual learning during the height of the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Locally heavy snow fell to the west of Thermopolis, with 7 inches of snow near Hamilton Dome. Amounts dropped sharply from Thermopolis and eastward.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread snowfall occurred over much of southeast Wyoming on the 21st through the 23rd. Interstate 80 was closed near Rawlins for an extended period of time until late on the 24th. Local emergency management reported local power outages along with numerous search and rescue operations to collect stranded motorists. COOP observer 1 mile north of Rawlins reported a storm total snowfall of 18 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across central Wyoming on July 26th and brought showers and thunderstorms that brought strong to damaging winds for portions of Fremont and Natrona Counties. The most intense storm occurred in Fremont County. There were numerous wind gusts over 58 mph and some gusts as high as 74 mph. Numerous limbs and trees were downed on the west side of Riverton, including at the Riverton Country Club. Wind gusts as high as 68 mph occurred in Natrona County as well as the line of thunderstorms moved eastward.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fast moving line of showers and thunderstorms brought strong winds to much of Natrona Counties. There were widespread reports of wind gusts past 58 mph, including a 68 mph gust along Wyoming Boulevard on the south side of Casper.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm over the area and put down and brought high wind from the Natrona County airport to Mills. Wind gusts as high as 68 mph were reported with the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 68 mph wind gust was reported near Mills. The Natrona County airport had a maximum wind gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm developed northwest of Sundance and tracked east-northeast into South Dakota. The storm produced large hail near golf ball size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture and a passing shortwave combined to produce to slow moving strong to severe thunderstorms across Natrona County. The most notable storm occurred in northern Natrona County to the south of Midwest. A thunderstorm dropped large amounts of hail up to quarter size along Highway 259, leaving up to a foot of hail on the road that made travel very difficult for several hours. The combination of hail and very heavy rain brought local flooding to that area as well, resulting in some dirt and gravel roads washing out. To the southwest of Casper, there was a brief touchdown of a rope tornado, but no damage was reported. Heavy rain also caused minor flooding in northwestern Natrona County later in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Video was posted to Facebook by a spotter of several inches of hail up to quarter size covering Highway 259 to the south of Midwest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large, slow moving low pressure system intensified rapidly as it tracked across the central Rockies between 4/2 and 4/5. This weather system combined with a historically significant amount of moisture from both the Pacific Ocean and Gulf of Mexico to produce extreme snowfall rates across portions of southeast Wyoming. Heavy snow initially developed across Carbon, Albany, Converse, and Niobrara counties during the overnight hours from 4/2 into 4/3, before spreading across the remainder of the High Plains late in the evening on 4/3. Accumulating snowfall persisted across much of the region until mid to late afternoon on 4/4. Total accumulations ranged from 1 to 2 feet for many areas along and north of a line from Rawlins to Wheatland to Lusk, with localized amounts exceeding 2 feet in higher elevation areas. In Carbon County near Rawlins and Elk Mountain, the snow combined with strong wind gusts near 45 MPH to produce periods of blizzard conditions. Significant transportation impacts were reported with extended (36+ hour) closures including both I-80 and I-25 west and north of Cheyenne, respectively.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports of 6+ inches of snow were received from the North Snowy Range Foothills  area near Elk Mountain. The public estimated up to 2 feet of accumulation near Arlington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large, slow moving low pressure system intensified rapidly as it tracked across the central Rockies between 4/2 and 4/5. This weather system combined with a historically significant amount of moisture from both the Pacific Ocean and Gulf of Mexico to produce extreme snowfall rates across portions of southeast Wyoming. Heavy snow initially developed across Carbon, Albany, Converse, and Niobrara counties during the overnight hours from 4/2 into 4/3, before spreading across the remainder of the High Plains late in the evening on 4/3. Accumulating snowfall persisted across much of the region until mid to late afternoon on 4/4. Total accumulations ranged from 1 to 2 feet for many areas along and north of a line from Rawlins to Wheatland to Lusk, with localized amounts exceeding 2 feet in higher elevation areas. In Carbon County near Rawlins and Elk Mountain, the snow combined with strong wind gusts near 45 MPH to produce periods of blizzard conditions. Significant transportation impacts were reported with extended (36+ hour) closures including both I-80 and I-25 west and north of Cheyenne, respectively.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple sources reported an average of 3 to 6 inches of snow across eastern Laramie County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong weather disturbance brought widespread precipitation to southeast Wyoming from late in the evening on 4/24 through the early morning hours on 4/26. Snowfall accumulations were restricted to the higher elevation areas, with most of the Sierra Madre, Snowy, and Laramie Ranges only seeing 3 to 6 inches of snow. Localized pockets of 6 to 8 inches were observed over the highest peaks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple SNOTEL sites in the Sierra Madre Range reported 48-hour liquid precipitation totals of 0.3 to 0.6 inch. Snow-to-Liquid ratios were estimated at approximately 10 to 1. Maximum snowfall totals were estimated near 6 inches over the highest peaks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture and a passing shortwave combined to produce to slow moving strong to severe thunderstorms across Natrona County. The most notable storm occurred in northern Natrona County to the south of Midwest. A thunderstorm dropped large amounts of hail up to quarter size along Highway 259, leaving up to a foot of hail on the road that made travel very difficult for several hours. The combination of hail and very heavy rain brought local flooding to that area as well, resulting in some dirt and gravel roads washing out. To the southwest of Casper, there was a brief touchdown of a rope tornado, but no damage was reported. Heavy rain also caused minor flooding in northwestern Natrona County later in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain and hail flowed to the west of Highway 257 and washed out dirt roads in Brown Draw.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of heavy rain and mountain snow melt brought minor flooding along the Wind River. The flooding remained in the low lying areas near the river and caused no property damage or road closure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred along the Wind River near Riverton. The flooding remained in low lying areas near the river and caused no damage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred along the Wind River near Kinnear. No damage or road closures occurred. However, water was close to running over Highway 132 due to a clogged culvert.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms initially developed over the higher terrain of the Laramie Range late in the afternoon on 6/21, rapidly increasing in both coverage and intensity as they tracked slowly eastward through the evening. This activity evolved into a large, slow moving cluster of moderate to heavy rain and embedded thunderstorms, with pockets of small to marginally severe (quarter to half dollar sized) hail associated with the strongest precipitation cores. Flash flooding was also observed in multiple locations due to the extended residence time of the higher rainfall rates.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter located 7 miles SW of Wheatland reported quarter sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved across northern Wyoming and brought heavy snow and strong wind to the northern third of the state that led to numerous transportation issues. In the mountains, the Jackson Hole Ski Resort picked up 25 inches of new snow. Across the much of the Bighorn Basin, reports of 6 to 10 inches was common. Although less snow fell in Johnson County, the snow was accompanied by wind gusts to almost 50 mph that led to the closure of Interstates 25 and 90 for a time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell around Thermopolis and Luzerne, with 6 to 10 inches measured through the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another Pacific trough and cold front moved into western Wyoming and brought heavy snow, especially to the mountains. The Tetons and Salt and Wyoming Ranges, were the hardest hit, with both locations seeing over 2 feet of new snow in some locations. Portions of Yellowstone Park and the western Wind River Range also saw over a foot of new snow. Very heavy snow also fell in the Star Velley, with amounts ranging from 10 inches at Afton to 16 inches near Thayne. The snow included snow squalls that dropped visibility to nearly zero for brief periods of time. In the eastern Wind River Range, a nearly stationary narrow band of snow brought over a foot of accumulation and resulted in the closure of Highway 28 over South Pass for several hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Favorable northwest flow brought heavy snow to the Star Valley. Amounts ranged from around 10 inches near Afton to 16 inches at Smoot.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow-moving low pressure combined with ample moisture from an atmospheric river brought heavy snow and strong wind to much of western and central Wyoming. The combination brought local blizzard conditions to portions of Johnson, Natrona, Sweetwater and Fremont Counties that led to multi-day closures of major roads. The heaviest snow fell in the western mountains where reports of over 20 inches of snow were common. The highest amounts fell in northwestern Wyoming where 40 inches fell in southwestern Yellowstone Park and over 30 inches at the Jackson Hole Ski Resort. Reports of over a foot of snow were commonplace in the western Valleys. East of the Continental Divide, over 18 inches of snow was reported in the Absaroka Mountains and Bighorns. In the populated lower elevations, the heaviest snow fell in the northeasterly upslope locations, with up to 14 inches around Casper and close to 20 inches in the foothills to the south of Lander. Across Sweetwater County, winds gusting to around 70 mph brought blizzard conditions that closed Interstate 80 for an extended period, with several people having to be rescued from stranded vehicles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell throughout the Jackson Valley, with amounts generally from 6 to 12 inches. The highest amount fell southwest of Wilson where 17 inches of new snow fell.

In the mountains, the Jackson Hole Ski Resort picked up 25 inches of new snow. Across the much of the Bighorn Basin, reports of 6 to 10 inches was common. Although less snow fell in Johnson County, the snow was accompanied by wind gusts to almost 50 mph that led to the closure of Interstates 25 and 90 for a time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late evening hours on February 24, a skier triggered avalanche occurred and deposited snow in Flat Creek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A skier-triggered avalanche occurred and deposited snow in Flat Creek. This snow created a temporary dam and lead to the creek rising out of its banks, bringing minor flooding to a townhome development and the parking lot of a gas station. Heavy equipment was called that night and dug a channel through the snow to release water from behind the avalanche-deposited snow dam. The creek was flowing again by 4 AM MST, and the water  returned to normal levels. No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tightening pressure gradient ahead of a strong low pressure system and strong mid-level winds mixing to the surface brought high winds to much of the Green Mountains and Natrona County. The highest wind gust reported was 84 mph at the Fales Rock RAWS site. Winds gusted over hurricane force in the wind prone areas south and east of Casper, with gusts of 83 and 80 mph reported along Hat Six Road and Wyoming boulevard, respectively. The strong wind brought blowing and drifting snow that resulted in road closures. The strong wind was followed by a snow storm that brought blizzard conditions at times. Please see the Winter Storm for details on that system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hurricane force wind gusts occurred in the higher elevations, with gusts to 84 mph at Fales Rock and 78 mph at Camp Creek. Wind gusted past 58 mph in the lower elevations as well, bringing the closure of US 287 for many hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another Pacific trough and cold front moved into western Wyoming and brought heavy snow, especially to the mountains. The Tetons and Salt and Wyoming Ranges, were the hardest hit, with both locations seeing over 2 feet of new snow in some locations. Portions of Yellowstone Park and the western Wind River Range also saw over a foot of new snow. Very heavy snow also fell in the Star Velley, with amounts ranging from 10 inches at Afton to 16 inches near Thayne. The snow included snow squalls that dropped visibility to nearly zero for brief periods of time. In the eastern Wind River Range, a nearly stationary narrow band of snow brought over a foot of accumulation and resulted in the closure of Highway 28 over South Pass for several hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Locally heavy snow fell in Yellowstone Park. A total of 13 inches of new snow fell to the west of the south entrance of the park.

Slow-moving low pressure combined with ample moisture from an atmospheric river brought heavy snow and strong wind to much of western and central Wyoming. The combination brought local blizzard conditions to portions of Johnson, Natrona, Sweetwater and Fremont Counties that led to multi-day closures of major roads. The heaviest snow fell in the western mountains where reports of over 20 inches of snow were common. The highest amounts fell in northwestern Wyoming where 40 inches fell in southwestern Yellowstone Park and over 30 inches at the Jackson Hole Ski Resort. Reports of over a foot of snow were commonplace in the western Valleys. East of the Continental Divide, over 18 inches of snow was reported in the Absaroka Mountains and Bighorns. In the populated lower elevations, the heaviest snow fell in the northeasterly upslope locations, with up to 14 inches around Casper and close to 20 inches in the foothills to the south of Lander. Across Sweetwater County, winds gusting to around 70 mph brought blizzard conditions that closed Interstate 80 for an extended period, with several people having to be rescued from stranded vehicles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in much of the northern portions of the Cody Foothills. Amounts around Cody generally ranged from 4 to 8 inches; but 19 inches was measured at Pahaska.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another Pacific trough and cold front moved into western Wyoming and brought heavy snow, especially to the mountains. The Tetons and Salt and Wyoming Ranges, were the hardest hit, with both locations seeing over 2 feet of new snow in some locations. Portions of Yellowstone Park and the western Wind River Range also saw over a foot of new snow. Very heavy snow also fell in the Star Velley, with amounts ranging from 10 inches at Afton to 16 inches near Thayne. The snow included snow squalls that dropped visibility to nearly zero for brief periods of time. In the eastern Wind River Range, a nearly stationary narrow band of snow brought over a foot of accumulation and resulted in the closure of Highway 28 over South Pass for several hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy snow fell at the Jackson Hole Ski Resort, with 28 inches of new snow reported at the summit. Amounts were generally less than a foot elsewhere.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A group of snowmobilers unintentionally triggered an avalanche in the Salt and Wyoming Range, about 15 miles south of Afton, above Poker Creek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 24 year old man on a snowmobile triggered an avalanche and was buried under 6 to 7 feet of snow. The riding party worked to dig him out, with the later assistance of Star Valley SAR, who arrived on scene shortly after a radio out for help. Sadly, the man did not survive the incident. More info can be found in the Bridger Teton Avalanche Center event summary, titled Poker Creek Avalanche Fatality; 2-23-2023.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved across northern Wyoming and brought heavy snow and strong wind to the northern third of the state that led to numerous transportation issues. In the mountains, the Jackson Hole Ski Resort picked up 25 inches of new snow. Across the much of the Bighorn Basin, reports of 6 to 10 inches was common. Although less snow fell in Johnson County, the snow was accompanied by wind gusts to almost 50 mph that led to the closure of Interstates 25 and 90 for a time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On top of the Jackson Hole Ski Resort, 25 inches of new snow fell. Amounts were much less in surrounding locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure crossed the state and brought heavy snow and high wind to portions of western and central Wyoming. The heaviest snow fell in the Tetons where up to 16 inches of new snow fell at the Jackson Hole Ski Resort. The Jackson Valley was also hard hit with 6 to 10 inches of snow. Moderate to heavy snow combined with strong wind as well. Wind gusted as high as 94 mph at Red Canyon along Highway 28, resulting in the closure of Route 28 over South Pass. A wind gust of 76 mph occurred at the top of the Jackson Hole Ski Resort. High wind also occurred in the wind prone areas of central Wyoming, with wind gusts over 70 mph around Casper and 66 mph around Buffalo.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On top of the Jackson Hole Ski Resort, 16 inches of new snow fell. High wind accompanied the snow, with a maximum wind gust of 76 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong upper low and trough moved out of the Great Basin area northeast across Wyoming during this time period. At the same time, a surface low moved across Wyoming into South Dakota. As a result of all the above, a deep, easterly upslope flow was in place across the Big Horn Mountains and adjacent foothills. During the first 24 to 36 hours of the event, conditions were unstable with steep lapse rates. This resulted in periods of heavy snowfall during the evening and overnight hours of the 24th and through the day on the 25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The mountains received 1 to 2 feet of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure crossed the state and brought heavy snow and high wind to portions of western and central Wyoming. The heaviest snow fell in the Tetons where up to 16 inches of new snow fell at the Jackson Hole Ski Resort. The Jackson Valley was also hard hit with 6 to 10 inches of snow. Moderate to heavy snow combined with strong wind as well. Wind gusted as high as 94 mph at Red Canyon along Highway 28, resulting in the closure of Route 28 over South Pass. A wind gust of 76 mph occurred at the top of the Jackson Hole Ski Resort. High wind also occurred in the wind prone areas of central Wyoming, with wind gusts over 70 mph around Casper and 66 mph around Buffalo.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow and blowing snow brought near zero visibility that resulted in the closure of Highway 28 over South Pass. A maximum wind gust of 94 mph occurred at Red Canyon with a prolonged period of wind gusts past hurricane force.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low passing over Wyoming combined with a strong jet stream to bring an outbreak of severe thunderstorms. Four tornadoes were reported on this day, the most in one day since June 12, 2017. There was one each in Johnson and Sweetwater County and two in Natrona County. The largest tornado was south of Midwest in northern Natrona County where a horse trailer was flipped and fuel tanks were blown away. Very large hail also occurred with some of the storms. The largest hail occurred in southern Johnson County and northern Natrona County where baseball size hail was reported. Another severe thunderstorms moved from Lander to Riverton and dropped quarter to golf ball size hail, causing damage to many vehicles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A rancher reported a brief touchdown of a tornado north of Kaycee to the Johnson County Sheriffs office.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tornadic thunderstorm developed over far southern Campbell County and tracked east-northeast into Weston County. The storm produced a large F-2 tornado that moved over the North Antelope Rochelle Coal Mine in far southeastern Campbell County, causing considerable damage and minor injuries at the mine. Large hail and wind gusts over 80 mph accompanied the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado continued from southeastern Campbell County into southwestern Weston County. It blew down some trees along a ridge, a tree at a residence, and some windbreak panels and ripped off part of a house roof. The width of the tornado through Weston County was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense, long-lived supercell initially developed near the town of Chugwater over the higher terrain of the central Laramie Range around 3:00 PM on 6/23, tracking to the east-southeast across southern Platte and Goshen Counties through approximately 7:00 PM. A small, terrain-driven area of low pressure and resultant cyclonic flow resulted in moisture pooling and backed, easterly near-surface flow near the track of the storm which resulted in a favorable environment for tornadoes. The cyclic supercell produced 5 separate confirmed tornadoes, most notably including an EF-2 tornado which developed near the town of Hawk Springs. A swath of large hail up to two inches in diameter also occurred with this storm. A separate storm over far northwestern Converse County also produced a brief tornado that afternoon. A second round of strong to severe thunderstorms impacted Platte and Goshen Counties later in the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained weather spotter reported golf ball sized hail along I-25 at Slater Road.

A long-lived supercell thunderstorm developed over the higher terrain of the central Laramie Range in far northwestern Laramie County near the Platte County late in the afternoon on 6/26. This thunderstorm quickly became severe as it tracked to the east-southeast across northern and eastern Laramie County through the early to mid evening hours, before exiting into western Nebraska. Multiple tornadoes were reported over rural eastern Laramie County, with an official NWS damage survey confirming two EF-1 tornadoes to the west and southwest of Albin, and an EF-0 tornado 8 miles ESE of Albin. In addition to the tornadoes, this storm also produced up to baseball sized hail near Gun Barrel. Additional isolated severe storms occurred over Converse County later in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An off-duty NWS employee found baseball sized hail on Highway 85 approximately 3 miles WSW of Gun Barrel after the storm passed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense, long-lived supercell initially developed near the town of Chugwater over the higher terrain of the central Laramie Range around 3:00 PM on 6/23, tracking to the east-southeast across southern Platte and Goshen Counties through approximately 7:00 PM. A small, terrain-driven area of low pressure and resultant cyclonic flow resulted in moisture pooling and backed, easterly near-surface flow near the track of the storm which resulted in a favorable environment for tornadoes. The cyclic supercell produced 5 separate confirmed tornadoes, most notably including an EF-2 tornado which developed near the town of Hawk Springs. A swath of large hail up to two inches in diameter also occurred with this storm. A separate storm over far northwestern Converse County also produced a brief tornado that afternoon. A second round of strong to severe thunderstorms impacted Platte and Goshen Counties later in the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large cone tornado developed just east of US-85 south of Hawk Springs, before quickly becoming rain-wrapped. The tornado snapped cottonwood trees, with a tree branch impaling a stucco house. It also damaged outbuildings and snapped power poles. An injury occurred when the tornado flipped an 18-wheeler and injured the driver. The same tornado tracked to the southeast and damaged another outbuilding and a barn 3 miles southeast of Hawk Springs. The tornado was officially rated an EF-2. In addition to the tornado damage, straight-line winds from the Rear Flank Downdraft (RFD) damaged several hardwood trees. This is consistent with 100 MPH winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long-lived supercell thunderstorm developed over the higher terrain of the central Laramie Range in far northwestern Laramie County near the Platte County late in the afternoon on 6/26. This thunderstorm quickly became severe as it tracked to the east-southeast across northern and eastern Laramie County through the early to mid evening hours, before exiting into western Nebraska. Multiple tornadoes were reported over rural eastern Laramie County, with an official NWS damage survey confirming two EF-1 tornadoes to the west and southwest of Albin, and an EF-0 tornado 8 miles ESE of Albin. In addition to the tornadoes, this storm also produced up to baseball sized hail near Gun Barrel. Additional isolated severe storms occurred over Converse County later in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A confirmed tornado began approximately 12 miles W of Albin at 6:42 PM MDT. The tornado damaged a single-family residence and destroyed a farm outbuilding. Minimal shingle damage was was visible at the home, with trees and power lines remaining intact on the property. Debris from the home and outbuilding was thrown several hundred yards to the southeast. Hay bales weighing up to 1400 pounds were moved between one quarter and one half mile from their original location. Further southeast along the track of the tornado, 8 wooden power poles were snapped. The damage then became more sporadic along the track as the tornado moved over mainly open fields before dissipating approximately 9 miles WSW of Albin at 6:50 PM MDT.

Multiple tornadoes were reported over rural eastern Laramie County, with an official NWS damage survey confirming two EF-1 tornadoes to the west and southwest of Albin, and an EF-0 tornado 8 miles ESE of Albin. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A confirmed tornado developed approximately 10 miles SW of Albin at 6:52 PM MDT. The tornado snapped 11 wooden utility poles, but otherwise caused little damage as it remained over open fields before dissipating around 7-8 miles SW of Albin at 7:05 PM MDT.

A small, terrain-driven area of low pressure and resultant cyclonic flow resulted in moisture pooling and backed, easterly near-surface flow near the track of the storm which resulted in a favorable environment for tornadoes. The cyclic supercell produced 5 separate confirmed tornadoes, most notably including an EF-2 tornado which developed near the town of Hawk Springs. A swath of large hail up to two inches in diameter also occurred with this storm. A separate storm over far northwestern Converse County also produced a brief tornado that afternoon. A second round of strong to severe thunderstorms impacted Platte and Goshen Counties later in the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ping-pong ball sized hail was reported 8 miles N of Chugwater via mPING.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Nearly stationary thunderstorms developed across portions of southeast Wyoming leading to flash flooding in the Central Laramie Range west of Wheatland as well as in eastern Laramie County with flooded and washed out roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Received a video on Facebook of flooded fields with water running over Carpenter Road just north of Road 208. Water over roadway appeared to be a least a foot deep.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long-lived supercell thunderstorm developed over the higher terrain of the central Laramie Range in far northwestern Laramie County near the Platte County late in the afternoon on 6/26. This thunderstorm quickly became severe as it tracked to the east-southeast across northern and eastern Laramie County through the early to mid evening hours, before exiting into western Nebraska. Multiple tornadoes were reported over rural eastern Laramie County, with an official NWS damage survey confirming two EF-1 tornadoes to the west and southwest of Albin, and an EF-0 tornado 8 miles ESE of Albin. In addition to the tornadoes, this storm also produced up to baseball sized hail near Gun Barrel. Additional isolated severe storms occurred over Converse County later in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was reported by an NWS Employee approximately 5 miles NNE of Whitaker.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave moved across central Wyoming and brought high winds to portions of Natrona and Fremont Counties. Around Riverton, wind gusts as high as 63 mph were reported. Wind gusts as high as 61 mph were reported on the south side of Casper.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts as high as 61 mph were reported on the south side of Casper.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front and shortwave moved across Wyoming. Showers and thunderstorms mixed strong low level winds to the surface and brought numerous reports of wind gusts over 50 mph. The highest wind occurred in the Wind River Range where favored channeling brought wind gusts as high as 96 mph. In the lower elevations, the highest wind gust was 80 mph to the north of Casper. Wind gusts over 58 mph occurred in Fremont, Teton, Natrona, Sublette and Sweetwater Counties. In Fremont County, the wind capsized a kayak and threw two men into the water at Ocean Lake. One man could not be rescued and was presumed drowned. On the north shore of Lake Yellowstone, a microburst downed hundreds of trees in the Lake Village area of the park, blocking roads and causing a loss of power for multiple days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 59 mph wind gust was reported at Farson.

On Ocean Lake near Riverton WY, a kayak was capsized and two men were thrown into the water. One was rescued by a nearby boater, but the other could not be found and was presumed dead by the Fremont County Sheriff (Sheriff Office press release from July 5, 2023).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave moved across Wyoming. As it encountered more humid air east of the Bighorn Range, severe thunderstorms developed in Johnson and Natrona Counties. There were numerous reports of hail from nickel to golf ball size hail, including on Interstate 25 which slowed traffic for a time. In portions of Fremont and Sweetwater Counties, strong winds mixed to the surface and brought local high wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail the size of nickels to golf balls fell from north of Natrona to the south of Midwest, including along Interstate 25.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There were two areas of severe thunderstorms this day. Around Rock Springs, high based thunderstorms moved into an area with an almost 60 degree dew point depression. A maximum wind gust of 62 mph occurred at the Sweetwater County airport. A line of thunderstorms associated with a shortwave also moved into Teton County. A 61 mph wind gust was reported at the Jackson Hole airport. In Wilson, many trees were downed, resulting in the loss of power for several hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A maximum wind gust of 62 mph occurred at the Sweetwater County airport.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hundreds of trees were downed in and around Wilson WY. Some trees landed on power lines, resulting in a loss of power for several hours in Wilson. Pictures shared by emergency management showed fallen trees which had damaged homes, businesses, and vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A gust front from thunderstorms to the north pushed southward across Thermopolis WY. Around a dozen trees were downed in Thermopolis, including two that were uprooted. Some trees and branches fell and damaged power lines. Winds were estimated to be around 50 mph. One tall structure had a metal roof which was peeled back by the strong winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 61 mph wind gust was reported at the Big Piney Airport. Tree damage was reported near Big Piney. A roof was peeled off a commercial warehouse in Marbleton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Near record snowpack began melting out during the month of May and brought widespread minor flooding to the Bear River in Lincoln County. This second flood wave continued into the month of June.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred in southern Lincoln County, with the most affected area along the Bear River near Pixley Dam. The floods were largely confined to low-lying agricultural land and a few dirt roads near the river. This second flood wave from melt in the mid elevations had a peak flow at the Bear River below Pixley Dam gauge reading of 1750 CFS, with the peak occurring June 1.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Near record snowpack began melting out during the month of May and brought widespread minor flooding to the Bear River in Lincoln County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred in southern Lincoln County, with the most affected area along the Bear River near Pixley Dam. The floods were largely confined to low-lying agricultural land and a few dirt roads near the river. This first flood wave from melt in the lower and mid elevations was the highest peak of the season, with peak flow at the Bear River below Pixley Dam gauge reading of 1760 CFS.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor impacts from flooding confined to low lying areas near the river. No damage was reported to transportation infrastructure or property. Maximum flow of around 3500 CFS occurred from May 25 through May 26 at the Salt River near Etna river gauge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system brought widespread snow and blowing snow to portions of southeast Wyoming, mainly along and west of the Laramie Range. Total snowfall accumulations of 3 to 6 inches were common for the lower elevations, with isolated maximum amounts of 6 to 10 inches over higher terrain. In the mountains, 1 to 2 feet of snow fell across the Snowy, Sierra Madre, and northern Laramie Ranges at elevations above 8000 feet. Dangerous winter conditions resulted in the closure of Interstate 80 between Laramie and Rawlins from the evening of 10/12 into the early morning hours on 10/13.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The official 24-hour NOHRSC gridded snowfall analysis ending at 5:00 AM MST on 10/13 indicates that widespread 3 to 6 inches of snow fell across lower elevations of northern Carbon County, with 6 to 12 inches in the mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure crossing Wyoming combined with deep moisture to bring locally heavy snow to portions of central Wyoming. The heaviest snow fell across Fremont County, where a band of heavy snow set up during the afternoon and evening hours of November 23rd. Snowfall rates reached 3 inches an our at times. There were numerous reports of over 16 inches from Lander to Riverton. Over 2 feet of snow fell outside of Lander. Amounts around Riverton ranged from 16 to 22 inches. At the COOP observer near downtown Riverton, a daily snowfall record of 19.2 inches was set. Over 6 inches of snow also fell in the lower elevations of Casper, Park, Natrona and Johnson Counties. Portions of the Wind River Mountains received over 2 feet of snow. The heavy snow and gusty wind led to road closures over portions of the area, including Highway 28 over South Pass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in portions of the area, especially from Hudson to Riverton where 12 to 21 inches of snow fell. Snowfall amounts dropped further north, with generally less than 6 inches around Shoshoni.

A long-lived supercell thunderstorm developed over the higher terrain of the central Laramie Range in far northwestern Laramie County near the Platte County late in the afternoon on 6/26. This thunderstorm quickly became severe as it tracked to the east-southeast across northern and eastern Laramie County through the early to mid evening hours, before exiting into western Nebraska. Multiple tornadoes were reported over rural eastern Laramie County, with an official NWS damage survey confirming two EF-1 tornadoes to the west and southwest of Albin, and an EF-0 tornado 8 miles ESE of Albin. In addition to the tornadoes, this storm also produced up to baseball sized hail near Gun Barrel. Additional isolated severe storms occurred over Converse County later in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A confirmed brief tornado overturned a pivot on CR 215 approximately 8 miles SSE of Albin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure brought deep moisture from the Gulf of Mexico and brought heavy snow to the mountains East of the Continental Divide, mainly above 8000 feet. The heaviest snow fell on the eastern slopes of mountain ranges. Over 3 feet of snow fell in portions of the Absarokas and the eastern Bighorn Range. Amounts of 1 to 2 feet also fell in on Casper Mountain, the eastern Wind River Range, and the western Bighorn Range. Below 7500 feet, most precipitation fell as rain, with only light accumulations on non-paved surfaces.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in the higher elevations of the western Bighorns. The highest amount was 16 inches at the Shell Creek SNOTEL site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weather system moved across Wyoming and brought heavy snow to portions of northern Wyoming. Some locations in northwestern mountains received 12 to 18 inches of new snow. In areas East of the Divide, an axis of heavier snow formed along a jet streak and brought locally heavy snow East of the Divide. Some locations around Dubois received around a foot of new snow with some locations in Big Horn County receiving 8 inches of snow. The heavy snow brought difficult driving conditions, especially over mountain passes.

The heaviest snow fell across Fremont County, where a band of heavy snow set up during the afternoon and evening hours of November 23rd. Snowfall rates reached 3 inches an our at times. There were numerous reports of over 16 inches from Lander to Riverton. Over 2 feet of snow fell outside of Lander. Amounts around Riverton ranged from 16 to 22 inches. At the COOP observer near downtown Riverton, a daily snowfall record of 19.2 inches was set. Over 6 inches of snow also fell in the lower elevations of Casper, Park, Natrona and Johnson Counties. Portions of the Wind River Mountains received over 2 feet of snow. The heavy snow and gusty wind led to road closures over portions of the area, including Highway 28 over South Pass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts around Dubois ranged from 6 to 12 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low passing over Wyoming combined with a strong jet stream to bring an outbreak of severe weather. Three tornadoes were confirmed in the WFO Riverton warning area on this day. This was the most in one day since June 12, 2017. There was one tornado in Sweetwater County and two in Natrona County. The largest and most impactful tornado was south of Midwest, WY in northern Natrona County where a home and surrounding property was damaged. Very large hail also occurred with some of the storms. The largest hail occurred in southern Johnson County and northern Natrona County where baseball size hail was reported from strongly rotating supercell thunderstorms. Other severe thunderstorms moved from Lander to Riverton and dropped quarter to golf ball size hail, causing damage to many roofs and vehicles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A supercell thunderstorm moved across northern Natrona County and brought very large hail to the south of Midwest. Hail almost baseball size fell in a farm to south of Midwest as well as along Interstate 25.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were numerous reports, pictures, and video of a cone tornado that touched down to the south of Midwest, WY. The tornado remained over open country for the most part. However, the tornado damaged one home where a large section of the roof was removed. Exterior walls remained standing. A trailer was overturned, fuel tanks were blown away, and trees near the residence were damaged.||Based on the FR12 damage indicator category, this tornado was rated EF1 using a low to middle end DOD estimate for the type of construction. Winds are estimated to have been around 110 mph. Stronger winds could have occurred elsewhere along the track, but open country prevented any further evidence of wind damage. Damage survey completed by county emergency management officials.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A microburst from a thunderstorm caused localized damage to a barn and outbuildings near Lucerne.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damage to a barn and several outbuildings from a strong thunderstorm wind gust at a ranch between Thermopolis and Kirby WY. Estimated wind gust of 50 to 60 mph based on damage. Damage estimated to have occurred around 426 PM MDT June 4 2023 based on radar. Nearest surface observation was a personal weather sensor 4 miles away, which showed a gust to 22 mph at the 445 pm reporting time, but storm did not directly affect this sensor location. Sheds, items, and debris were blown east to west generally with no indication of distinct rotation. Outbuildings had openings to the east, making them especially susceptible to easterly wind gusts. Little to no damage to nearby farms or trees, as the downdraft was from a small storm, leading to very localized damage, likely from an individual downdraft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On June 15th, roughly between 300 PM and 415 PM MDT, slow moving thunderstorms fueled by a wet air mass led to heavy rain over parts of Natrona County, including the city of Casper, WY and surrounding suburbs. Rainfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour were common for many locations. These high rainfall rates combined with other factors, such as wet antecedent conditions, hilly topography, and urban impermeable surfaces resulted in the flash flood event that afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall reports for the 24 hour period including the flash flood event ranged from 1.30 to 3.47 inches. Flooding was initially reported in downtown Casper where cars where were reported as floating in flowing water. On the south side of Casper, a house was reported as shifted off of its foundation. Interstate 25 became flooded at the peak of the event, and was closed for until well after rainfall ended. Low-lying tunnels on the south side of Casper had as much as 5 feet of water in them at the height of the storm. Damage values have been updated as of Oct 2023 and are from the FEMA presidential disaster declaration, FEMA-4739-DR-WY. No injuries or fatalities were reported to the NWS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tornadic thunderstorm developed over far southern Campbell County and tracked east-northeast into Weston County. The storm produced a large F-2 tornado that moved over the North Antelope Rochelle Coal Mine in far southeastern Campbell County, causing considerable damage and minor injuries at the mine. Large hail and wind gusts over 80 mph accompanied the storm.

The storm produced a large F-2 tornado that moved over the North Antelope Rochelle Coal Mine in far southeastern Campbell County, causing considerable damage and minor injuries at the mine. Large hail and wind gusts over 80 mph accompanied the storm.

A tornadic thunderstorm developed over far southern Campbell County and tracked east-northeast into Weston County. The storm produced a large F-2 tornado that moved over the North Antelope Rochelle Coal Mine in far southeastern Campbell County, causing considerable damage and minor injuries at the mine. Large hail and wind gusts over 80 mph accompanied the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado developed near the Campbell-Converse County line southeast of Wright, where it snapped wooden power poles and tipped over several empty train cars. It struck the operations area of the North Antelope Rochelle Coal Mine, ripping metal roofs and wall panels from shop buildings; moving and flipping cars, buses, and shipping containers; and overturning empty railroad cars. Damage severity indicated wind gusts were as high as 120 to 130 mph. No track was visible across the vegetation. Seven people sustained mostly minor injuries at the mine. The tornado continued into southwestern Weston County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds with a bora wind event returned to much of southeast Wyoming during the morning and early afternoon hours on December 7th. Wind-prone areas observed cross winds gusting up to 76 mph north of Cheyenne along Interstate 25. Portions of Interstate 80 and 25 were closed to light weight and high profile vehicles for an extended period of time on the 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CWOP mesonet station DW7426 Douglas measured maximum wind gust 5 miles west-southwest of Douglas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cutoff upper level low across the central US began retrograding westward with wrap around moisture and strong winds leading to heavy snowfall and blizzard conditions, especially along the Pine and Cheyenne Ridges in eastern Wyoming. Snowfall totals along the western extent of the Pine Ridge reached 6 inches near Lusk with near-blizzard conditions. Further south in eastern Laramie County, strong winds gusting 50 to 60 mph led to low visibility and blizzard conditions with the heavy snowfall. Cheyenne observed a brief period of blizzard conditions early morning on the 26th while the Interstate 80 Summit between Cheyenne and Laramie observed blizzard conditions for an extend period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported 4 to 6 inches of snow 10 miles northeast of Cheyenne with near-blizzard conditions. Cheyenne Aiport ASOS station (KCYS) reported blizzard conditions with one-quarter mile visibility and 35 to 50 mph wind gusts for 90 minutes early in the morning on 12/26.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cutoff upper level low across the central US began retrograding westward with wrap around moisture and strong winds leading to heavy snowfall and blizzard conditions, especially along the Pine and Cheyenne Ridges in eastern Wyoming. Snowfall totals along the western extent of the Pine Ridge reached 6 inches near Lusk with near-blizzard conditions. Further south in eastern Laramie County, strong winds gusting 50 to 60 mph led to low visibility and blizzard conditions with the heavy snowfall. Cheyenne observed a brief period of blizzard conditions early morning on the 26th while the Interstate 80 Summit between Cheyenne and Laramie observed blizzard conditions for an extend period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: AWOS station Alpha (Burns) - K1AW reported one-quarter mile visibility in falling and blowing snow 9 miles south-southeast of Gun Barrel with wind gusts 50 to 55 mph. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extremely active weather pattern remained in place across the central Rockies during this time period, mainly characterized by a parade of weather disturbances embedded within an unusually strong mid and upper-level jet stream. This weather pattern resulted in persistent moist orographic ascent over the higher terrain of the Snowy and Sierra Madre mountain ranges, leading to an extended period of very heavy mountain snowfall. SNOTEL observations suggest 4 to 6 feet of snow fell in the mountains over this 72-96 hour period. In addition to the mountain snow, the elevated flow aloft combined with strong pressure gradients and strong subsidence resulted in multiple waves of strong winds with gusts 65-75 MPH, initially starting in the southeast Wyoming wind corridors before eventually spreading into the adjacent valleys and high plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple SNOTEL sites suggest a total of 3 to 5 feet of snow fell in the Sierra Madre Range in the 3-day period from 12/1 to 12/4. A maximum estimated total of 54.8 inches was recorded at Little Snake River (LITW4), 12 miles SW of Encampment at an elevation of 8928 feet.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous WYDOT mesonet stations along Interstate 80 reported wind gusts around 60 mph. WYDOT mesonet station Vedauwoo (KVDW) reported maximum wind gust at 10:32 PM MST on 12/25.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system moving through the region resulted in a landspout forming in southwest Wyoming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: LANDSPOUT. Location: 40.17, -110.44. Public recorded a video of a landspout tornado from County Road 283 facing west in Robertson, WY. Approximate location of the landspout tornado was near the intersection of County Road 274 and County Road 279. Any damage associated with the landspout is unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense, long-lived supercell initially developed near the town of Chugwater over the higher terrain of the central Laramie Range around 3:00 PM on 6/23, tracking to the east-southeast across southern Platte and Goshen Counties through approximately 7:00 PM. A small, terrain-driven area of low pressure and resultant cyclonic flow resulted in moisture pooling and backed, easterly near-surface flow near the track of the storm which resulted in a favorable environment for tornadoes. The cyclic supercell produced 5 separate confirmed tornadoes, most notably including an EF-2 tornado which developed near the town of Hawk Springs. A swath of large hail up to two inches in diameter also occurred with this storm. A separate storm over far northwestern Converse County also produced a brief tornado that afternoon. A second round of strong to severe thunderstorms impacted Platte and Goshen Counties later in the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm chaser located 4 miles N of Chugwater reported ping-pong ball sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low over Wyoming brought a deep, upslope flow resulting in heavy snow to the Northeast Big Horn Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 2 to 3 feet occurred in the mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe storms impacted part of southeast Wyoming the afternoon of 29 June 2023.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Received video and text report from Twitter of golf ball sized hail at intersection of 4th and Reynolds in Laramie.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated thunderstorm produced quarter size hail 7 miles north of Sheridan.

From the afternoon of July 6 on into the evening, isolated to scattered clusters of showers and thunderstorms impacted portions of northern Utah and southwest Wyoming, yielding a few strong to severe wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The I80 - Leroy mesonet station recorded a period of wind gusts to 65 mph as a result of outflow winds associated with a nearby cluster of showers and thunderstorms moving through the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following scattered convection throughout the afternoon hours, a more cohesive squall line formed over Utah's West Desert region. This squall progressed eastward through the northern portions of Utah during the evening, and subsequently pushed into southwest Wyoming thereafter. This squall resulted in a swath of strong to very strong wind gusts, due in part to low dew points and dry low levels of the atmosphere. The same pattern responsible for the strong widespread squall event also yielded isolated strong wind gusts the morning of July 4 with additional decaying convection.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A school crossing sign was blown off of a pole.
